questions.q: In haloalkanes, the bond angle is generally
questions.explanation: In haloalkanes, the bond angle is generally less than 109°28' due to the presence of a halogen atom, which is a polar bond and creates a slight deviation in the bond angle.

questions.q: The type of isomerism exhibited by 1-bromo-2-fluoropropane is
questions.explanation: 1-bromo-2-fluoropropane exhibits position isomerism because the bromine and fluorine atoms are in different positions on the alkyl chain.

questions.q: The reaction of haloalkanes with NaOH is an example of
questions.explanation: The reaction of haloalkanes with NaOH is an example of SN2 reaction, where a nucleophile replaces the halogen atom.

questions.q: The stability of haloarenes is generally due to
questions.explanation: The stability of haloarenes is generally due to resonance stabilization, where the delocalization of electrons leads to a more stable molecular structure.

questions.q: The reaction of haloarenes with KOC2H5 is an example of
questions.explanation: The reaction of haloarenes with KOC2H5 is an example of electrophilic substitution, where the ethoxide ion acts as a nucleophile and the benzene ring acts as an electrophile.

questions.q: The compound 1,2-dibromobenzene is an example of
questions.explanation: 1,2-dibromobenzene is an example of an ortho-para director, which means that the incoming nucleophile will substitute at the ortho or para position.
questions.topic: Directing Effects in Electrophilic Aromatic Substitution

questions.q: The reaction of haloalkanes with AgNO3 is an example of
questions.explanation: The reaction of haloalkanes with AgNO3 is an example of a double displacement reaction, where the halide ion is displaced by the nitrate ion.

questions.q: The compound 1,3,5-trinitrobenzene is an example of
questions.explanation: 1,3,5-trinitrobenzene is an example of a meta director, which means that the incoming nucleophile will substitute at the meta position.

questions.q: The reaction of haloarenes with CuCl2 is an example of
questions.explanation: The reaction of haloarenes with CuCl2 is an example of electrophilic substitution, where the copper(II) ion acts as an electrophile and the benzene ring acts as a nucleophile.

questions.q: The compound p-bromotoluene is an example of
questions.explanation: p-bromotoluene is an example of a monosubstituted benzene, where one hydrogen atom has been replaced by a bromine atom.

questions.explanation: The reaction of haloalkanes with NaI is an example of SN2 reaction, where a nucleophile replaces the halogen atom.

questions.explanation: 1-fluoro-2-methylbenzene is an example of an ortho-para director, which means that the incoming nucleophile will substitute at the ortho or para position.

questions.explanation: The reaction of haloarenes with NaOH is an example of nucleophilic substitution, where the hydroxide ion acts as a nucleophile and the benzene ring acts as an electrophile.

questions.q: The compound 1,2,3-trichlorobenzene is an example of
questions.explanation: 1,2,3-trichlorobenzene is an example of a trisubstituted benzene, where three hydrogen atoms have been replaced by chlorine atoms.

questions.explanation: The reaction of haloalkanes with KCN is an example of SN2 reaction, where a nucleophile replaces the halogen atom.

questions.explanation: p-chlorobenzonitrile is an example of a monosubstituted benzene, where one hydrogen atom has been replaced by a chlorine atom.

questions.explanation: The reaction of haloarenes with H2O is an example of nucleophilic substitution, where the water molecule acts as a nucleophile and the benzene ring acts as an electrophile.

questions.q: The reaction of haloalkanes with PCl5 is an example of
questions.explanation: The reaction of haloalkanes with PCl5 is an example of SN1 reaction, where a strong electrophile replaces the halogen atom.
